author: @ChaplainDaveSparks
text:
RIP, sir! 

The one thing that the plant pot heater did that this one doesn't is to act as a _"heat bank"._ The plant pot had a lot more *_thermal mass_* than the stainless steel cylinder, meaning that it would continue heating the room after the flame went out, which might be useful to use a more powerful heat source, such as a _Trangia burner._ You could burn it for a while to get the pot hot, then extinguish it. For example, in a bedroom, you could heat the pot up, then extinguish the burner before going to bed. Ditto for multiple candles.

author: @tonyhardy-kp4os
text:
There should be another variable in getting a clean burn other than only air vs fuel ratio, i am thinking about temperature. If we make the design of the burner to burn at higher temperatures then we can get a hotter cleaner burn without any kind of forced air or electric blowers needed. 
For example: for the burner we can make something very similar to your infrared radiator but instead of using the infrared to heat the person this infrared reflector will be used to heat the combustion area. But we certainly still need that infrared radiator for the person, but we need one for the combustion too. So the bottom line, we could use a tiny tin around the combustion flame with adequate air holes for combustion and then place another slightly larger nd taller tin around that tin(with adequate air openings) to ensure enough mass there to store and radiate more heat in the combustion area.  
I seen this ideal working on a kerosene heater, it has a metal center that fits around the wick, when you first light it you have to wait to turn up the wick until that center metal center to heat up or else it will spew soot. Once it is hot then you can turn it up full blast(expose more fuel/wick) because now the combustion area is hot so now it will still burn clean. NOTE: it burns a little soot in the beginning when lit from a cold start no matter what, about 30 seconds it stops completely tapering off as it warmer and warmer and it is hot.

author: @FrogFWK
text:
There are several things to consider, when building these things. 

First, make sure no toxic substances are released from the coating/metal surface while heating. You can use a gas cooker outdoors and burn everything off the surface that could release harmful vapors when used indoors. The result wouldn't look as appealing, but it's worth it. Better use old metal baking tins (the "bread formfactor" can be used horizontally), which were designed for high temperatures anyway. Make sure no wooden/flameable furniture is close during operation.  

Second, when the metal reaches 57°C within a few second, it's likely to max out at 170-250°C until the balance of energy input/output is reached (depending on heat source). This creates a serious burning hazard, as everything above 70-80°C is too hot to handle. This is why the original plant pot designs are multilayered with at least one inner and one outer Pot, which doesn't exceed 70°C, when fueled correctly. For more output build a bigger one, which can handle more separated candles. Each candle has to be separated from its neighbour to prevent gas ignition. Always have a bowl filled with sand as a fire extinguisher at hand.

Third, a good design would combine fast heat distribution through a metal structure inside to a much larger outer object such as a pot with a large surface area, which provides heat radiation in the most possible even and consistent way - without a large heat gradient from top to bottom. The inner metal objekt works as a diffusor and the outer body is the actual radiator.

Fourth, every diy heating system has its limits in terms of physical characteristics and safety. So don't overpower it, better build another one or two (or more) and place them around the heatet area. Weaker/less candles are safer to use. With a bit of experience/trial & error you can build a kind of heat curtain around your place in a 180° angle, which can be a desk or a sofa etc. Remember to adapt your security arrangements, when small kids or animals are in the house.
Hope this helps, cheers.

replies.author: @sinisamilisavljevic8833
replies.text:
Meanwhile I did some experimenting (to avoid bothering you with the question, hehe):
Obtained some plant pot 25 cm deep, 28 cm wide on top, and turned it up side down on some wire stand.
Made one copper coil alcohol burner and lighted it up under the pot.
The coil diameter was 4 cm, and the flame was a bit bigger than that, about 1 cm wide.
After one hour the pot temperature was roughly 60 Celsius.
Tomorrow, from the very similar starting temperature I've turned a 200 Watt incandescent bulb on under the pot instead of the burner.
After one hour the pot temperature was around 70 Celsius.
Didn't try with a set of four tea candles.
~~~~~
The whole thing is perfect for heating small tent in your room in case of problems with your main heater or the fuel supply.
I would say that bulb is at least a bit safer than a burner.
~~~~~
The heater was burning about 5.5 hours (300 ml of 96% ethanol), which costed about 1 Euro (3.3 Euro per liter at my local pharmacy).
As a secondary heat source it saved me about 0.65 Euro on the gas boiler for that day.
5 hours of the bulb saved me about the same 0.65 Euro of the daily gas for the price of One kilo Watt hour (5hr x 200W) which is 8 cents.
In both cases my thermostat was set to the same 19 Celsius.
~~~~~
If I obtain heating cable (10 meters, at some 20W/m) my next experiment will be with the 200W cable directly on the stone tiles under the carpet where I usually sit.
The cable I have in mind is self-regulating at 65 degrees, flat, which will give pretty usable thermal contact with the tiles.

author: @RaptorFlyer3D
text:
Hi Robert, I’m new to your channel and particularly took inspiration from this video as I’ve often thought about making my own plant pot heater. 

My design consists of an 8” diameter Panettone tin which is 7 1/4” tall. It is inverted so that the tea light candles can sit within the lid and act as a tray. 
I initially punched 4 small holes around the side, just above the lid’s lip but I noticed there wasn’t enough airflow. So I increased the quantity of holes to 15 which are just under 2” apart. This still wasn’t enough so I got my step drill out and cut 7 1”holes whose centres are 2” from the bottom. This seems to be working well now. I’m currently running 3 tea lights inside and it is giving a surface temperature in the middle of the upper surface at 114 degsC. The sides are warm to touch but not hot enough to burn me. 
I’m going to add a 4th candle to see if it improves the output  and see how it fairs. 

Does anyone have any further suggestions for improvements please?

Update:
Adding the 4th candle takes the upper surface temp to 130degsC.

replies.author: @evanreed5347
replies.text: My current design for unhoused folks is a wick made of carbon felt also rolled with steel mesh to give it a wide burn area and some extra support and then I wrap it in a piece of copper wire to give it a foot and hold the rolled wick upright. Make sure your rolled wick touches the bottom of your container and sticks up at least 1.5 cm above the fue line. I put that in a metal cup of cooking oil and plant the metal cup in a plant pot full of sand as big as you want to move around.  I cut some wire fence panel into 2 pieces 1 for my chimney/mantle/burn chute and one for my wind break reflector... I leave some extra fence wire sticking out so that they can be rolled up into 2 rings and staked down in the pot. The first ring should fit fairly tightly around the rim of your metal fuel container.  Once it's staked in your plant pot cut a piece of steel mesh that will the space between the top of your ring and the rim of your fuel cup when it is rolled up and slide it in the ring. Roll and stake your outer ring to the same level or a little higher than the first ring. Cut a piece of foil that can be folded 3 or 4 times to add strength and wrap around the outer ring about 3/4ths of the way around to make a wind break and reflector. Cut sone extra fence material to make a grill top. Add some rubbing alcohol to your cup and wick and light it up. Ip put a cast iron skillet on top as a radiator and cooking surface big enough to keep rain from getting in the mesh burn chamber and set it outside in the California bomb cyclone and it worked for about 3 hours on a cup of oil with no maintenance. Heat light cooking and wind and water resistant... it actually helps to keep the sand or soil around your metal container wet to help spread the heat away from the fuel container.  Without a heat sink of some type that doesn't get steam in your burn chute tge oil will get too hot and boil so violently that it blows itself out while getting hot oil all over the place... the burner or multiple could be planted in a garden  with a more permant stove/oven set up using bricks and rocks and colored glass pieces and different pots and tops add nice fun variation and personalizing... I call it the Hurricane Hibachi or the Seed of Life... less than $1 to make with some hunting for materials (buy the carbon felt!!!!) And only requires scissors and wire cutters to build. THANK YOU ROBERT!!!! You made this possible too!!!! I am teaching some groups at a local cidery how to make them and hand out my first 100 Seeds next weekend. Cheers, Sir Smith please build one and show it off if you feel inclined 🥂

author: @nico-bf1kr
text:
It gives me an idea for the wick to get more contact with air that i will try to describe. 
imagine a metallic tube in a little bit larger one, filled with the wick in between, so that the center is empty.
about a centimeter down the top , square windows or round holes are drilled through so that air can acces inside the inner pipe while there is still enough wick between the windows to suck the fuel by capillarity. 
when the wick will be alight it will suck the air through that holes since air goes up in a warm pipe.
=> and therefore we get more contact with air.
Plus on the top of the tubes we make crenelations (like on top of walls of fortified castles) so that not all the perimeter of the wick is alight ( => need less air) and that again air can come through the side of each flame to feed the fire. i think it can be easy to make crenalation just by pinching the tube together...
eventually this mimic a gaz stove burner.

author: @Berkana
text: Robert, there is something this heater is not doing optimally. Shiny surfaces have low emissivity in the infrared spectrum. If you shot-peen or sand-blast the surface and blacken it, it will radiate heat much more efficiently. A shiny material will simply not emit as much radiant heat as a dull material at the same temperature.

replies.author: @chrislau9921
replies.text: This refer a little bit to my question if any material has its own unique ir radiation spectrum? So if you say shiny surfaces have low emissivity in the infrared spectrum, that could just mean for my understandig that it has to do something with surface area. Beacuse until the matiral not starts to glow in the visual spectrum it must give up all its additional energy in the infrared spectrum. That means that you could optimize the heater if it would radiate about 70Watts of infrared heat. I thing this could be done by weight and/or surface area.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @Berkana
replies.text:
@chrislau9921 It isn't a spectrum like a line spectrum, but a black-body spectrum. Do you know about black body radiation? It's the spectrum emitted by an object due to its temperature. Everything above absolute zero has some level of emission of photons, and the shape of that spectrum tends to have a certain look. The more shiny and reflective things are, the worse they are at emitting black-body radiation. This is not about the surface area or weight; this is about how emissive a material is. The general trend is that shiny and reflective things are poor emitters, while dull and light-absorbing things are good emitters.

Look up black body radiation on Wikipedia; the entry is pretty good. The shape of the spectrum depends on the temperature, but the intensity of the emissions depends on the emissivity.

author: @jamest.5001
text:
You would be better off making a mini brick fireplace , from paver brick, putting the candles or alcohol burners inside, 
Use epoxy to glue the brick together, or use strapping or large clamps to hold it together! You can drill holes in brick pavers, 
But a small brick fireplace, not much larger than a shoe box, could be a cool decorative heater! Depends on how far you want to go!   I have thought about building a near ful size one in my house!  With an opening 12"-15" tall, and 18" wide, with a brick and half on each side, with a cement board and air gap behind it, with aluminum foil on the wall, with aluminum tape reflecting any heat that may get through the board, then stack the brick to build the chimney, use a 2" exhaust pipe to fit through the wall, using a short piece of 3" pipe to fit tight through the wall, with the 2" with 90° bend fitting through the wall about 6' high, then going up to the gutter, above it about 3' with a rain hat, then epoxy the brick together covering the pipe, use a bit of sheet metal to shroud the fire box,  paint the cement board black, or grey high temp paint, it being only about 6" deep,  it can hold  the can methanol burners,  or kerosene burners,  make a small butterfly to close off the 2" pipe when needed,  with alcohol it would not need the exhaust pipe,  but the brick will hold the heat, depending on how bug you want to go, it could use 2 dozen brick, or about 100 for what I want to build,.  But brick or ceramic tiles can make a nice little planter pot alternative,  some tile come in 18"x18" maybe even 24"x24"  two of those , and a few paver brick, and a bit of epoxy, you can cut the shape out of the tile, probably at the home supply,  it may require a diamond disc for a single grinder, and a sponge, a bucket of water, to keep it wet and cool,

author: @malHHkenny
text:
Here's lot to ask -- and yet would be so satisfying to apply:

I only wish we had metrics on wattage/joules/calories applied to thermal radiation/conduction/convection required to warm whatever dimensions of a (well insulated) space and/or a person in that space. I'd love to see that equation, but I'd much better understand a chart of the variables. (You've been so good as to providing the energy output data on fuels.)

replies.author: @ianclarke8821
replies.text: Air is roughly 1.25kg per cubic metre. Specific heat capacity, near enough to 1 so a 4 x 4 x 2m room therefore 32 cubic metres. 40kg. It takes one joule to heat 1g (of air, 4.3 times as much for water) through 1 degree Celsius per second. Your super candle - let’s say 150 watts (150 joules per second) will therefore take this room from 10 degrees to 20 (being a bit simplistic and ignoring density changes in expansion) in 40,000 x 10 / 150 /  60 minutes - roughly 44 mins, but that totally ignores heat loss through walls ceiling etc. So in reality an equilibrium between heat supplied and lost is reached. Old house may never get above a few degrees over ambient temperature. Super modern AAA house, maybe but probably more than 90 minutes. Have a go - let us know!

replies.author: @malHHkenny
replies.text: @ianclarke8821 I'll work through this data and see how processes can be applied. Thank you for providing. (I'll also convert to Fahrenheit and feet/inches for my own convenience/familiarity and figure a space that's 6 feet 8 inches wide x 8 feet long x 8 feet high -- roughly van-RV interior dimensions, with well above average insulation. [I can measure loss by timing drop from the current-heater-produced 70 degrees inside to level with 45 degrees outside at night in this part of California lately.])

replies.author: @ianclarke8821
replies.text: @malHHkenny your dimensions and required heat rise give 23 minutes at zero loss using 150 watt flame - a biggish flame or 2 and a bit candles! In reality it will of course be longer - not only due to heat loss, but you’ll want to breathe too! So reckon on good ventilation. My guesstimate is about an hour… good luck, don’t suffocate or poison yourself!

replies.author: @malHHkenny
replies.text: @ianclarke8821 Rise, and then there'd be maintenance of temp over time. Loss could also be another measure with ventilation happening -- but then, who can say what the data is for accurately mitigating toxicity? A separate category on the same chart could indicate the energy/heating requirements as a fully electrical process -- and possibly translated to combustion wattage as data equivalence, right? Funny how I can get data as therms (similar to BTUs), for my house, by looking at my utility bill (gas and electricity are two separate utilities where I live), but anything for any space smaller is a mystery.

text:
I may be repeating what others have said- but if a contained wax or oil burner raises the temperature of the wax to flash point then whoosh!
Another factor is if bits of match or any other matters such as crane flies & moths serve as extra wick the temperature can rapidly rise faster than can escape by convecting and radiating away. If the candle is near anything flammable it can rapidly spread. It can also spit globs of fire if encountering drops of water (moth juice).
This is the similar principle as a chip pan fire except a naked flame is already present.
You could deliberately explore this in a safe place in the garden - but either let it burn out or lid/smother it, Dont use water or try to carry extremely hot wax.
Awareness of danger is consciously responsible safety.
But we are not all educated to any practical degree of responsibility - but towards a sealed unit with no user access.

author: @felipearbustopotd
text:
Heating the Human.. 

Yet another great uploads by RMS.

Atm I am finding that this keeps me cozy. 

I use a tall non combustible container apx 2 foot / 24 inches in height and apx 8 inches in diameter... just imagine a saucepan but with 2 foot high sides.

In the base a place a tea light - which is then lit, the rim adorns a contoured wire mesh  - this prevents anything from falling into the base and potentially causing a fire - that whole set up is then placed inside a sleeping bag. My 1st attempt was with a blanket wrapped around my legs - which also did the trick.

I then place my legs inside and try to eliminate any gaps. Any heat that rises is trapped in the small environment and hey presto - warmth.

As the set up is not hermetically sealed the chance of the tea light being snuffed out if very remote. 

I have tried using more than 1 tea light but have found it can get too hot. 1 tea light suffices.


As with all naked flames...  great caution is advised

author: @trplankowner71
text: I still haven't found out if paraffin wax in a stock pot on your wood burning stove actually undergoes a phase change, or is it a different process.

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: Any substance that turns from one form, solid or liquid or gas, to another goes through a phase change.  With rare exceptions, the energy gained or lost during the phase change is far far greater than non phase change temps.  So everything has this dynamic including wax.  Now there are diff paraffins which have different melting or evap temps.  So just pick one that will melt on a stove at that temp.  You can Google melting temps for different paraffins.  If it is completely melted into a liquid, it will have x amount of watts of energy stored in it in relation to ambient temps.  As the paraffin hardens it will release heat.  Then you can heat it up again until it's all melted.  However, if you burn paraffin like a candle 🕯️, it will evaporate into the air.  Hope that helps and answers your question?

replies.author: @trplankowner71
replies.text: @l0I0I0I0 Yes, I remember freshman chemistry. However, some molecules appear to undergo a phase change, yet actually haven't. Glass comes to mind. Paraffin wax is a very long chain hydrocarbon molecule and is quite soft as a "solid", yet water is a small, but polar molecule and is quite hard as a solid. Possibly something else might be going on when paraffin wax "solidifies". It may well only look solid. there might be temporary bonds of some sort between those long chain molecules. My knowledge of organic chemistry isn't in depth enough to know. I have a lot of nuclear physics heaped on those chemistry memories.

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: @trplankowner71 Then you probably already know that paraffin of all materials  had the lowest expansion ratios of all phase change materials, meaning it can be kept contained without breaking out of a container as it changes phases.  With the exception of liquid to gas, where it is extremely volatile and will explode as it has an exceedingly high expansion ration as it turns into gas and will explode especially when it hits air and can ignite.  But as long as the temp does not reach the gas state, it's fine to work with.  Water however is so amazingly high in it capacity to both hold and release heat, but most interesting is it's thermal heat capacity, I'm surprised someone has not yet developed a reliable and safe way to use water for heating and cooling AND in conjunction with heat storage, as well as cooling storage?  Of course lol cooling storage is the absence of heat but your a physicist and know what I mean.  Lol

author: @martineleven8179
text: You could do the same process with a glass jar candle,  render down 2 blocks of lard with 1 fifth beeswax until melted,  for your wick use some 4mm natural jute rope soaked in the fuel,  attach it to a ring pull from a drink can and secure it to the bottom of the jar,  pour fuel when cool,  when cured trim wick to about 1/4 inch.   Works really well but you will have to trim wick every 3 hrs or so  ,   lard only costs 40p a block,  much cheaper than anything,  adding beeswax roughly doubles cost but all in all still cheaper than buying paraffin candle.  Lasts longer too.

replies.author: @pattiannepascual
replies.text:
I would never trust burning crisco in the cardboard boxes they come in these days. it's just paper with thin foil inside.
I remember in the old days,crisco came in actual metal cans. 
To be safe, melt the crisco,pour into heavy jars,or metal cups,let cool and then put the candle inside. 
much safer. If you use the crisco container and the candle gets warm and topples to the side, that entire paper container will catch on fire.

author: @Struthio_Camelus
text: I remember seeing a plant pot heater that you covered with graphite. The idea was that the graphite would help the efficiency of the IR radiation. Would the shiny stainless steel then decrease the efficiency of the radiation?

replies.author: @deemdoubleu
replies.text: You asked the same question for me thanks, but according to what I have read a matt black surface would emit more efficiently, so you would think that the surface temp of the heater would be lower when running - is that correct?  Also, you could use exhaust paint for the paint job - my gas engineer told me to spray my discoloured coals on my gas fire with it.  Perhaps someone more knowledgeable can chip in.

replies.author: @davenewland4675
replies.text: @deemdoubleu I don't know about more knowledgeable but I have been burnt by my polished aluminum pressure cooker. I put my hand near it to check if it was hot. Feeling no heat, I touched it to verify. Yup it was hot - ouch. Reading about emissivity will answer some questions. A perfect radiator has a value of 1. Polished stainless is about 0.07 and polished aluminum about 0.04 so very good at keeping the heat in. That heat has to eventually go somewhere so it is still a heater but the temperature of the metal might be higher than if it was painted (or if it was a flower pot!).

replies.author: @deemdoubleu
replies.text: @davenewland4675 Yes I think the idea is to transfer as much heat energy as possible into/as radiated heat emission rather than heating the air close to the surface by conduction and then causing more air convection.  Perhaps it's all swings and roundabouts.

replies.author: @Struthio_Camelus
replies.text:
@deemdoubleu In the States we have a high-temperature paint called stove-black, used for wood burning cast-iron stoves and gas grills. 
It's a matte-black coating but I don't know if it has a practical advantage beyond the beautification of the heater.

replies.author: @Useaname
replies.text:
Most cheap toilet brush holders aren't chromed anyway. They're just stainless steel. Chroming adds extra cost to the manufacturing process. Also, chrome has a nice silver mirrored finish, whereas polished stainless steel has a grayer mirrored finish, sometimes not highly polished if you're talking about the really cheap ones. 
So it's easy to tell.
